guest_credentials: World Tour cycling coach at Team Bora-Hansgrohe, formerly at UAE Team Emirates and Science to Sport, specializing in endurance and ultra-distance training
key_claims: Training for ultra-endurance events should prioritize neuromuscular efforts and metabolic work on the bike, with significant off-bike strength training from day one

Sustainable power output in a comfortable position is more valuable than aggressive aerodynamic positioning that cannot be held; a rider doing 220W for the full duration in non-aggressive position beats an aggressive position they cannot sustain
Cadence drops progressively with fatigue in ultra events; athletes eventually switch from higher cadence/power to lower cadence/higher torque (newton meters) to continue forward progress
A three-week-on, one-week-off (3+1) or four-week-on, one-week-off (4+1) periodization structure is standard, with reverse periodization rarely used except in 1-2 specific cases

The submaximal fatigue test (3-minute effort at threshold to 110% threshold power, performed every 7-10 days) combined with RPE, time to exertion, sleep, mood, wellness, and body weight tracking provides near-bulletproof feedback on whether training stimulus is correct or if athlete is fatigued
specific_numbers: 190 watts average power (Rob Britain winning Badlands)
3-minute submaximal fatigue test at threshold to 110% threshold power
3+1 or 4+1 periodization (3-4 weeks on, 1 week off)
cluster_reasoning: John Wakefield's expertise centers on training methodology, periodization structures (3+1, 4+1), neuromuscular training, submaximal fatigue testing, and sustainable power output — all core coaching pillars; strength training is secondary because off-bike S&C is mentioned as foundational but not the episode's primary focus.
